message.content:
You are verifier #1, independent of all other verifiers. Do NOT confirm findings optimistically — only confirm if you can independently verify the issue exists in the actual code at /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay.

Finding F-026: "Concurrent test and single-threaded tests mutate os.environ directly without monkeypatch"
File: tests/test_persistence.py (line 476)
Description: Tests call os.environ["ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL"] = ... directly, bypassing monkeypatch. Single-threaded tests leave ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL permanently mutated, polluting subsequent tests.
PoC: Run two tests sequentially that set os.environ directly. Second test sees first test's value if monkeypatch.delenv not called.

Instructions:
1. Read the actual file at /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/tests/test_persistence.py
2. Look at the code at line 476 and surrounding context
3. Independently verify whether the described problem EXISTS in the current code
4. Check if the PoC is reproducible from the actual code as written
5. Consider whether this is already fixed (pass-1 #101-#122, pass-2 #124-#156 fixed many issues)
6. Return your independent verdict

Confirmed = true ONLY if: (a) the code problem exists as described, (b) the PoC is reproducible from current code, (c) it is a genuine production-readiness concern. Set confirmed=false for speculative issues, issues already fixed, or issues where the PoC does not match what the code actually does.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

464	def test_migration_lock_is_bounded(fake_pg):
465	    # #129: the advisory lock must be acquired under a bounded lock_timeout so a stuck peer
466	    # holding it cannot hang bootstrap forever. set_config('lock_timeout', ...) runs before the
467	    # pg_advisory_lock on the same connection.
468	    cp, state = fake_pg
469	    cp.get_checkpointer()
470	    assert state["lock_timeout_on"], "no lock_timeout set before advisory lock (#129)"
471	    # the configured bound is a positive integer-millisecond value
472	    (params,) = state["lock_timeout_on"]
473	    assert int(params[0]) > 0
474	
475	
476	def test_concurrent_init_of_distinct_conn_strs_does_not_serialize(fake_pg, monkeypatch):
477	    # #129: a stuck advisory-lock wait on conn_A must NOT block bootstrap of an unrelated conn_B.
478	    # We make conn_A's advisory lock block on an Event; conn_B (different conn_str) must still
479	    # complete. The pre-#129 code held a single process-global mutex across the advisory wait, so
480	    # conn_B would be blocked behind conn_A — this test discriminates that regression.
481	    import os
482	    import sys
483	    import threading
484	
485	    cp, state = fake_pg
486	    release_a = threading.Event()
487	    a_in_lock = threading.Event()
488	
489	    BlockPool = sys.modules["psycopg_pool"].ConnectionPool
490	
491	    class BlockingConn:
492	        def __init__(self, conn_str):
493	            self.conn_str = conn_str
494	
495	        def execute(self, sql, params=None):
496	            if "pg_advisory_lock" in sql and "5432/assay" in self.conn_str:
497	                a_in_lock.set()
498	                release_a.wait(5)  # hold the lock until released
499	
500	    class _PoolWrap(BlockPool):
501	        def __init__(self, conn_str, **kw):
502	            super().__init__(conn_str, **kw)
503	            self._conn = BlockingConn(conn_str)
504	
505	    monkeypatch.setattr(sys.modules["psycopg_pool"], "ConnectionPool", _PoolWrap)
506	
507	    results: dict = {}
508	
509	    def init_a():
510	        os.environ["ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL"] = "postgresql://localhost:5432/assay"
511	        cp.get_checkpointer()
512	        results["a"] = True
513	
514	    def init_b():
515	        a_in_lock.wait(5)  # ensure A is parked inside its advisory-lock wait
516	        os.environ["ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL"] = "postgresql://localhost:5433/other_db"
517	        cp.get_checkpointer()
518	        results["b"] = True
519

519	
520	    ta = threading.Thread(target=init_a)
521	    tb = threading.Thread(target=init_b)
522	    ta.start()
523	    tb.start()
524	    tb.join(5)
525	    assert results.get("b") is True, "conn_B blocked behind conn_A's advisory wait (#129)"
526	    release_a.set()
527	    ta.join(5)
528	    assert results.get("a") is True
529	
530	
531	def test_atexit_pool_cleanup_registered_once(fake_pg):
532	    cp, state = fake_pg
533	    import os
534	
535	    cp.get_checkpointer()
536	    os.environ["ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL"] = "postgresql://localhost:5433/second"
537	    cp.get_checkpointer()  # distinct conn_str → a second pool
538	    # two pools opened and tracked, but only one shared atexit handler registered
539	    assert len(state["pools"]) == 2
540	    assert len(cp._OPEN_POOLS) == 2
541	    assert state["atexit_registered"] == 1

547	    # exercise the race (each thread finishes the bootstrap section before the next is scheduled),
548	    # so we WIDEN the cache-empty→setup→cache-write window with a small sleep inside setup — this

content.text:
The evidence is clear. Let me confirm the specific lines in question:

1. Line 459: `os.environ["ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL"] = ...` (no monkeypatch)
2. Lines 510, 516: `os.environ["ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL"] = ...` inside threads (no monkeypatch)
3. Line 536: `os.environ["ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL"] = ...` (no monkeypatch)

The `fake_pg` fixture at line 352 does use `monkeypatch.setenv("ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL", ...)` at line 435, which means the fixture sets it — but the mutations at lines 459, 510, 516, and 536 bypass monkeypatch and write directly to `os.environ`. These values are NOT cleaned up after the test since they aren't tracked by monkeypatch's teardown mechanism.

The test at line 476 uses `monkeypatch` but the threads inside it (lines 510, 516) set `os.environ` directly — monkeypatch cannot track mutations made inside threads.

The test at line 531 (`test_atexit_pool_cleanup_registered_once`) sets `os.environ` directly at line 536 with no cleanup.

452	    cp, state = fake_pg

454	    cp.get_checkpointer()
455	    assert len(state["pools"]) == 1  # one pool reused, not two (#144)
456	    assert len(cp._OPEN_POOLS) == 1
457	    import os

460	    cp.get_checkpointer()  # distinct conn_str → its own pool
461	    assert len(state["pools"]) == 2
462

content.text: The finding is confirmed. Line 459 is in `test_pool_is_cached_and_reused_per_conn_str` which does NOT use monkeypatch at all, so the direct mutation to `os.environ` at line 459 permanently changes the environment for any subsequent tests that read `ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL` without their own setup.

input.reasoning:
The code at the described locations directly mutates os.environ without monkeypatch:

1. /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/tests/test_persistence.py line 459: `test_pool_is_cached_and_reused_per_conn_str` does not accept monkeypatch at all, so `os.environ["ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL"] = "postgresql://localhost:5433/other"` permanently mutates the process environment with no cleanup.

2. Line 536: `test_atexit_pool_cleanup_registered_once` similarly calls `os.environ["ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL"] = "postgresql://localhost:5433/second"` with no monkeypatch tracking.

3. Lines 510 and 516 inside `test_concurrent_init_of_distinct_conn_strs_does_not_serialize` (line 476): the threads `init_a` and `init_b` each set `os.environ["ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL"]` directly. Although the outer test accepts monkeypatch, monkeypatch cannot track assignments made from worker threads.

The `fake_pg` fixture (line 435) sets the env var via `monkeypatch.setenv`, which will restore it after the fixture scope — but all three direct mutations happen AFTER or OUTSIDE of monkeypatch tracking, so they will not be cleaned up by monkeypatch teardown. Any test that runs after these and reads `ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL` without explicit setup will see a polluted value. This is a genuine test-isolation bug that can cause non-deterministic failures depending on test ordering.
